Job continued to defend himself regarding his complaints. Besides external troubles, a deep sense of God's wrath took away all his courage and strength. The feeling of God’s wrath is harder to bear than any external suffering. What then did the Savior endure in the garden and on the cross, when he bore our sins, and his soul was offered as a sacrifice to divine justice for us! Whatever burden of affliction, whether physical or financial, God chooses to place upon us, we may well submit to it as long as he continues to give us the use of our reason and the peace of our conscience; but if either of these is disturbed, our situation is very pitiful. Job reflected on his friends for their criticisms. He complained that nothing was offered for his relief except what was tasteless, loathsome, and burdensome.

Job had desired death as a happy end to his miseries. For this, Eliphaz rebuked him, but he asks for it again with more intensity than before. It was very reckless to speak of God destroying him in this way. Who, for one hour, could endure the wrath of the Almighty if he unleashed his hand against them? Let us rather say with David, “Spare me a little.” Job based his comfort on the testimony of his conscience, that he had been, in some measure, helpful to the glory of God. Those who have grace within them, who have evidence of it and exercise it, have wisdom which will help them in the worst of times.

During his prosperity, Job formed great expectations from his friends, but now he was disappointed. He compares this to streams drying up in summer. Those who place their expectations on created things will find them fail when they should help them; whereas those who make God their confidence have help in time of need. Those who put their hope in gold will eventually be ashamed of it and their confidence in it. It is wise for us to cease relying on human beings. Let us put all our confidence in the Rock of ages, not in broken reeds; in the Fountain of life, not in broken cisterns. The application is very close: “for now you are nothing.” It would be well for us if we always had such convictions about the emptiness of created things as we have or will have on a sickbed, a deathbed, or in times of conscience trouble. Job rebukes his friends for their harsh treatment. Though he was in need, he desired no more from them than a kind look and a kind word. It often happens that even when we expect little from people, we receive less; but from God, even when we expect much, we receive more. Although Job differed with them, he was ready to yield as soon as it appeared that he was wrong. Even if Job had been in fault, they should not have treated him so harshly. He holds fast to his righteousness and will not let it go. He felt that there had not been such wickedness in him as they supposed. But it is best to commit our reputation to Him who keeps our souls; on the great day every upright believer will receive praise from God.
